perturbation.original_text: This Consulting Agreement shall be governed by, and construed pursuant to the laws of the State of New York, applicable to agreements made and performed wholly within such State. Any disputes under this Consulting Agreement shall be resolved by appropriate proceedings in the state of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Consulting Agreement shall be governed by, and construed pursuant to the laws of the State of New York, applicable to agreements made and performed wholly within such State. Any disputes under this Consulting Agreement shall be resolved through binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'appropriate proceedings in the state of New York' with 'binding arbitration,' the agreement attempts to force arbitration, potentially waiving the consultant's right to pursue claims in court. Without explicitly stating the waiver of jury trial rights, this may not be enforceable under New York law and potentially violates the consultant's right to pursue legal action in a court of law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 7503
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 7503 outlines the procedure for compelling or staying arbitration.  New York courts require a clear, explicit, and unequivocal agreement to arbitrate, particularly regarding the waiver of the right to sue in court.  The modified text implies arbitration without a clear waiver, creating potential grounds for a challenge to the enforceability of the arbitration clause, possibly undermining the right to access courts.

Application to compel or stay arbitration; stay of action; notice of intention to arbitrate. (a) Application to compel arbitration; stay of action. A party aggrieved by the failure of another to arbitrate may apply for an order compelling arbitration. Where there is no substantial question whether a valid agreement was made or complied with, and the claim sought to be arbitrated is not barred by limitation under subdivision (b) of section 7502, the court shall direct the parties to arbitrate. Where any such question is raised, it shall be tried forthwith in said court. If an issue claimed to be arbitrable is involved in an action pending in a court having jurisdiction to hear a motion to compel arbitration, the application shall be made by motion in that action. If the application is granted, the order shall operate to stay a pending or subsequent action, or so much of it as is referable to arbitration. (b) Application to stay arbitration. Subject to the provisions of subdivision (c), a party who has not participated in the arbitration and who has not made or been served with an application to compel arbitration, may apply to stay arbitration on the ground that a valid agreement was not made or has not been complied with or that the claim sought to be arbitrated is barred by limitation under subdivision (b) of section 7502. (c) Notice of intention to arbitrate. A party may serve upon another party a demand for arbitration or a notice of intention to arbitrate, specifying the agreement pursuant to which arbitration is sought and the name and address of the party serving the notice, or of an officer or agent thereof if such party is an association or corporation, and stating that unless the party served applies to stay the arbitration within twenty days after such service he shall thereafter be precluded from objec

perturbation.justification: The change from 'appropriate proceedings in the state of New York' to 'binding arbitration' removes the consultant's right to pursue claims in court. The Law Explanation, referencing CPLR § 7503, states New York courts require a clear, explicit, and unequivocal agreement to arbitrate, especially regarding the waiver of the right to sue in court. The altered text implies arbitration without explicitly stating the waiver. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing SECTION 7503 Application to compel or stay arbitration; stay of action; notice of intention to arbitrate, shows that the right to proceed in court is the default absent an agreement to arbitrate. Because the modified text weakens the consultant's right to pursue legal action in a court of law by implying forced arbitration, it contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT'S REPRESENTATIONS & WARRANTIES. Consultant represents and warrants to the Company as follows: The Consultant (i) has adequate means of providing for the Consultant's current needs and possible personal contingencies; (ii) is acquiring the Shares for investment and not with a view to their distribution and has no need for liquidity in this investment; (iii) is able to bear the substantial economic risks of an investment in the Shares for an indefinite period; and (iv) is an "accredited investor" as defined in Rule 501 of Regulation D of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended. Consultant is not under any legal obligation, including any obligation of confidentiality or non-competition, which prevents Consultant from executing or fully performing this Consulting Agreement, or which would render such execution or performance a breach of contract with any third party, or which would give any third party any rights in any intellectual property which might be developed by Consultant hereunder; and Consultant shall perform the Services hereunder in accordance with the terms of this Agreement and applicable regulations, guidelines and licensing requirements; and Consultant has all requisite power and authority to execute, deliver, and perform this Agreement, and this Agreement has been duly authorized, executed and delivered by Consultant and is Consultant's legal, valid and binding obligation, and is enforceable as to Consultant in accordance with its terms. The Consultant certifies, under penalties of perjury that the Consultant is not subject to backup withholding as a result of a failure to report all interest or dividends, or because the Internal Revenue Service has notified the Consultant that the Consultant is no longer subject to backup withholding, and that Consultant has provided the Company an accurate taxpayer ID number.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT'S REPRESENTATIONS & WARRANTIES. Consultant represents and warrants to the Company as follows: The Consultant (i) has adequate means of providing for the Consultant's current needs and possible personal contingencies; (ii) is acquiring the Shares for investment and not with a view to their distribution and has no need for liquidity in this investment; (iii) is able to bear the substantial economic risks of an investment in the Shares for an indefinite period; and (iv) is an "accredited investor" as defined in Rule 501 of Regulation D of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended. Consultant is not under any legal obligation, including any obligation of confidentiality or non-competition, which prevents Consultant from executing or fully performing this Consulting Agreement, or which would render such execution or performance a breach of contract with any third party, or which would give any third party any rights in any intellectual property which might be developed by Consultant hereunder; and Consultant shall perform the Services hereunder in accordance with the terms of this Agreement and applicable regulations, guidelines and licensing requirements; and Consultant has all requisite power and authority to execute, deliver, and perform this Agreement, and this Agreement has been duly authorized, executed and delivered by Consultant and is Consultant's legal, valid and binding obligation, and is enforceable as to Consultant in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the Consultant's certification under penalties of perjury regarding backup withholding creates a potential issue with IRS regulations. Companies are required to obtain this certification to ensure proper tax reporting and avoid penalties. Omitting this certification shifts the risk of potential tax liabilities to the company.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S.C. § 3406 deals with backup withholding due to notified payee underreporting of reportable interest or dividend payments. The omitted certification helps the company comply with this section by ensuring they have the correct taxpayer identification number and that the consultant is not subject to backup withholding. Failure to obtain this certification can lead to penalties for the company.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the Consultant's certification under penalties of perjury regarding backup withholding. The Law Explanation states that 26 U.S.C. § 3406 addresses backup withholding, and the omitted certification helps the company comply by ensuring the consultant is not subject to backup withholding. The Perturbation Explanation states that removing this certification shifts the risk of tax liabilities to the company. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions section '§3406. Backup withholding' and clauses related to payee failures and incorrect TINs. Omitting the certification directly contradicts the company's ability to ensure compliance with 26 U.S.C. § 3406.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any provision of this Consulting Agreement is determined by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unenforceable, the parties will negotiate in good faith to restore the unenforceable provision to an enforceable state and to provide reasonable additions or adjustments to the terms of the other provisions of this Consulting Agreement so as to render the whole Consulting Agreement valid and binding to the fullest extent possible, and in any event, this Consulting Agreement shall be interpreted to be valid and binding to the fullest extent possible.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any provision of this Consulting Agreement is determined by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unenforceable, the remaining terms and conditions shall remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that requires the parties to 'negotiate in good faith to restore the unenforceable provision to an enforceable state,' the contract eliminates a mechanism for addressing unenforceable clauses. While the remaining terms still remain in effect, the omission removes the obligation to attempt to rectify the situation through good-faith negotiation. This may contradict contract law principles that favor interpretations upholding the parties' original intent as much as possible and may not hold up in court, as it disincentivizes parties from finding common ground when a clause is found unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-1103 discusses agreements modifying or waiving contract provisions. While it allows for such modifications, New York courts generally favor interpretations that give effect to the parties' intent and uphold the contract. Removing the requirement for good-faith negotiation makes it more difficult to argue that the parties intended to maintain their original agreement as much as possible, potentially leading a court to find the agreement unenforceable if a key provision is struck down and no attempt was made to rectify it. This is a contradiction because it goes against the spirit of upholding contract validity.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1103 Written agreement for modification or discharge General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 11 § 5-1103. Written agreement for modification or discharge. An agreement, promise or undertaking to change or modify, or to discharge in whole or in part, any contract, obligation, or lease, or any mortgage or other security interest in personal or real property, shall not be invalid because of the absence of consideration, provided that the agreement, promise or undertaking changing, modifying, or discharging such contract, obligation, lease, mortgage or security interest, shall be in writing and signed by the party against whom it is sought to enforce the change, modification or discharge, or by his agent.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated good-faith negotiation to restore unenforceable provisions, aligning with the principle of upholding the parties' original intent. The modified text removes this obligation, potentially leading to unenforceability if a key provision is struck down and no attempt is made to rectify it. This omission undermines the principle of upholding the contract, as explained in the Law Explanation, creating a contradiction. The scraped snippets, specifically NY GOL § 5-1103, discusses written agreements for modification, and while allowing modifications, the omission removes a crucial step to maintaining the original agreement's spirit, thus contradicting the law's intent to give effect to the parties' original intentions.
